Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I'd block out time to study, maybe 22 hours a week on paper, but I rarely followed a consistent schedule. Most of my real studying happened late at night when my anxiety was keeping me awake anyway. I'd print out readings and work through them with a highlighter, making detailed notes in my own shorthand. The routine existed more in my head than in any actual timetable.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how overwhelmed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then I'd have these intense bursts where I'd study for hours straight before an exam. Deadlines definitely drove my engagement more than any internal schedule. When my stress levels spiked, I'd sometimes freeze up completely for days before forcing myself back into it.
Q3: Usually it was panic, if I'm being honest. I'd log in when I suddenly remembered something was due or when I needed to check if I'd missed an announcement. Sometimes I'd log in just to download materials so I could work offline. The notification emails would prompt me too, though I often let those pile up in my inbox before actually acting on them.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine the way it probably should have. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more regularly, but then life would get overwhelming and I'd avoid it for days. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped me manage my anxiety a bit, but they didn't really translate into more consistent LMS use.
Q5: The reading materials and lecture notes were invaluable to me because I could download them and study in my own way. I'm very much a read-write learner, so having text I could annotate, highlight, and rewrite in my own words was essential. The discussion posts were occasionally useful when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of. Videos were okay but I preferred having written content I could control the pace of.
Q6: I barely touched the videos most of the time, even though I know they were supposed to be helpful. Watching them felt passive and my anxious mind would wander constantly. I also ignored a lot of the optional readings and supplementary materials because I was already struggling to keep up with the core content. The peer interaction features felt awkward to me, so I only engaged when absolutely necessary.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam content or assignments. I know that sounds mercenary, but with my anxiety levels, I had to be strategic about where I spent my limited mental energy. If something helped me understand a concept I knew would be tested, I was all in. Activities that felt like busywork or weren't clearly tied to my grade got pushed aside.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, mentally speaking. Required ones carried this weight of obligation that would add to my stress, but at least I knew I had to do them. With optional stuff, I'd think 'I should do this' but then the anxiety would convince me I didn't have the capacity. It's frustrating because I know optional activities could have helped, but my brain couldn't prioritize them.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and print them out, then spend hours with physical notes spread across my desk. I have notebooks filled with rewritten concepts, summaries, and practice problems. I also used textbooks from the library and some external resources I found online. The LMS was basically just a delivery system for materials I'd study elsewhere.
Q10: My exam scores probably tell this story best - I got a 93.96 despite my low LMS engagement. I'd download a lecture's worth of material in one quick session, then spend days working through it offline with my notes and textbooks. I'd rewrite key concepts multiple times, create my own study guides, quiz myself on paper. None of that showed up anywhere in the system, but it's where my real learning happened.
Q11: All the time. I'd log in once, download everything available, and then not return for days or even weeks. My computer has folders organized by week with all the course materials I'd work through at my own pace. I found it less stressful to have everything locally where I could access it without internet issues or the pressure of being 'watched' by the system.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where engaging felt impossible. My anxiety would spike and I'd catastrophize about falling behind, which ironically made me avoid the course entirely. When I had competing deadlines from my four online courses, I'd sometimes just shut down. The mindfulness intervention helped me recognize these patterns, but it didn't always prevent them from happening.
Q13: Massively. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, the LMS felt like this mountain I couldn't climb. I'd open it, see all the things I hadn't done, feel my chest tighten, and close it again. Competing responsibilities from my other courses and extracurriculars meant I was constantly triaging. The LMS became something I'd only face when I absolutely had to, not something I engaged with regularly.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd open materials and stare at them while my mind raced with anxious thoughts about everything else I needed to do. Sometimes I'd scroll through content without actually processing any of it. Those sessions felt like going through the motions - I was technically 'engaged' but nothing was sticking. It was performative studying.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even though the anxiety about them was always there in the background. The perfectionism that comes with my anxiety made starting feel impossible - if I couldn't do it perfectly, why start at all? I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but that better feeling rarely came. My assignment completion rate suffered because of this avoidance pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have this surge of panic-fueled productivity in the hours before something was due. My LMS activity probably showed these intense spikes right before deadlines followed by radio silence. It wasn't healthy, but the deadline pressure was often the only thing stronger than my avoidance instincts.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. Good feedback would give me a temporary boost, but critical feedback could send me into an anxiety spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. I'd sometimes delay looking at grades or comments because I was scared of what they'd say. When I did get positive feedback, it surprised me and motivated me to engage more, at least briefly.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming at times, with so many different components to track across four online courses. I appreciated having materials available to download, but the pacing expectations didn't match my reality. The structure assumed consistent engagement, but my mental state made that impossible. I ended up creating my own structure focused on exams rather than following the intended weekly flow.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement easier because I could study in my preferred way. What made it harder was the expectation of regular online participation - discussion posts and peer interactions felt forced and anxiety-inducing. The sheer volume of content across my courses made it hard to keep up. Clearer connections between activities and assessments would have helped me prioritize better.
Q20: I think those students might be like me in some ways - logging in frequently out of anxiety, checking things obsessively, but then freezing when it comes to actually producing work. The perfectionism paralysis is real. Or maybe they're engaging with content but struggling to translate that into completed assignments. High activity doesn't mean high productivity when anxiety is involved.
Q21: That completely reflects my experience. My exam score was excellent despite barely showing up in the LMS because I was doing intensive work offline. I think some students are just more efficient or have different learning styles that don't require constant platform engagement. The LMS activity metrics don't capture someone like me who downloads everything once and then studies independently for hours.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they're online. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and all the offline work. They can't tell if someone is anxiously scrolling without absorbing anything versus deeply engaged. They miss printed materials, handwritten notes, study groups, textbook use. They capture presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense to me. It's about that moment when concepts click and I can explain them in my own words. For me, engagement happens when I'm rewriting notes, making connections between ideas, testing myself. It's not about time spent online - it's about mental effort and actual understanding. True engagement often happens in silence, with a pen and paper.
Q24: The LMS would never show the hours I spent with printed readings spread across my floor, color-coded and annotated. It wouldn't capture the anxiety attacks before logging in or the relief after exams. It can't see that my low engagement scores coexist with genuine intellectual curiosity and hard work. Most importantly, it misses that I'm struggling with my mental health while still managing to perform well academically. That gap between my visible activity and my actual learning is huge.
